articles.title: The Second Advent of the Messiah
articles.text:
Lesson Department
Theology and Testimony
LESSON 8

THE SECOND ADVENT OF THE MESSIAH

Helpful References

In the Bible:
Psalms 50:3-5; Isaiah 35:4; 40:10; Joel 2:28-32; 3:15-17; Zechariah 4:3-5; Malachi 3:1-3; Matthew 16:27; 24:3-6, 14, 29-30, 36-40; 25:31-32; Luke 21:10-17; Acts 1:9-11; 3:19-21; 1 Thessalonians 4:14-17; 2 Thessalonians 1:7-10; 2:1-5.

In the Book of Mormon:
3 Nephi 26:3; 28:7; 29:2.

In the Doctrine and Covenants:
29:9-21; 33:17-18; 34:6-9; 35:15-18; 45:37-57; 64: 23-24; 65:3-5; 88:87-99; 101:23-35; 110:14-16; 128:24; 130:1, 14-17; 133.

In the Pearl of Great Price:
Moses 7:59-67. See also An Extract from a Translation of the Bible.

Smith, The Way To Perfection, chapter 39.
Talmage, Jesus the Christ, chapter 42.

1. The Church Proclaims the Doctrine of the Second Advent.—A lesson on the teachings of the Christ respecting His Second Advent is timely. A cursory examination of the history of our Church makes plain the fact that the Prophet Joseph Smith expressly taught the doctrine of the Second Coming. Clearly he meant that Christ was to come in the relatively near future. He testifies plainly that heavenly messengers had so informed him. The revelations of the Lord to the Church seem to indicate that we cannot work too hard in making preparations for, and in warning the world of, the Christ's coming in glory.

2. Modern Disbelief in the Doctrine of the Second Advent.—Latter-day Saints do well to notice the unfortunate but growing tendency among men to disbelieve in the Second Coming of the Christ. It is part of our work to make careful observation of conditions in the world and to adjust our preaching to meet them.

Dr. Klausner, the great Jewish scholar, speaks of the Second Coming of the Savior as follows: "According to this idea, the Messiah must suffer and die, but he would in the end rise again and, this second time, appear in glory and establish the kingdom of heaven. To make this idea appear true the disciples stole the body of Jesus and hid it; after fifty days—by which time the body must have become unrecognizable even if found, they spread the rumor that he was risen from the dead and that he had shown himself alive to them. Thenceforward they awaited his Second Coming (Parousia), when he should establish his kingdom, the everlasting kingdom of heaven. And this Coming, rather than the ethical teaching of Jesus, became the fundamental hope and basis of early Christianity. All at first believed in his speedy coming; but when there seemed no prospect of an early coming they allocated it to a later age, to the close of a thousand years (the Millennium)."

Those who disbelieve in the Lord's Coming point out that such doctrine is nothing but a carry over from the beliefs of an earlier age when it failed of accomplishment and was accordingly put off for fulfillment at a much later time. To believe in it now, in the light of historical research, they say, is to exercise credulity rather than faith.

We believe that scholars and intelligent laymen alike, in many instances, have been misled in their belief. Their sincerity we do not question. Not having a sufficient understanding of the principles of the Gospel, they have jumped at erroneous conclusions.

3. The Ancient Prophets Understood the Doctrine of the Second Advent.—The Church teaches that the Gospel was in the world from the beginning, and that the coming of the Christ in the flesh was foreseen; His death and resurrection were predicted long before His advent in the flesh. Likewise, prophets and seers foresaw that His Second Advent in glory would take place long after His death and resurrection. Thus, Enoch, who lived centuries before the Christ's earthly advent saw in vision His Second Coming, as related in the Pearl of Great Price. "And it came to pass that Enoch saw the day of the coming of the Son of Man, in the last days, to dwell on the earth in righteousness for the space of a thousand years." (Moses 7:65; read carefully Moses 7:59-67). The Old Testament prophets understood the true teaching respecting the coming of the Messiah in glory. Joel the Prophet expressly refers to it in Chapter 2:28-32. The Prophet Joseph Smith relates that Moroni made reference to this prophecy when he appeared to him September 21, 1823. "He also quoted the second chapter of Joel, from the twenty-eighth verse to the last. He also said that this was not yet fulfilled, but was soon to be." (Paragraph 41, Extracts From The History of Joseph Smith, the Prophet, Pearl of Great Price). Since Joel speaks of "the terrible day of the Lord," he can scarcely have had reference to anything but the Second Coming. Malachi certainly had reference to it in his prophecy. (See Malachi 3:1-3 and compare Doctrine and Covenants, Sec. 128:24). Numerous other Old Testament references can be cited. (See references at beginning).

4. The Jews of Christ's Time Misunderstood Prophetic References to the Second Advent.—Unfortunately, the people of the Jewish nation at Christ's time had generally misunderstood the teachings of the prophets in reference to the Second Coming. Doubtless this was due to the fact that the nation had been taught too long by an uninspired or apostate priesthood. At any rate the Jews were awaiting a Messiah who was to come in power and rescue them from their enemies. Little did they suspect that many of the teachings they read in the prophetic books had reference to what we know as the Second Coming. The fact that the Christ was first to come into the world as a humble child had been practically forgotten.

5. The Savior Taught His Apostles the True Doctrine of His Second Coming.—It became necessary for the Savior to carefully explain to His apostles the true doctrine respecting His Second Advent. Shortly before He took Peter, James and John on the Mount of Transfiguration He said, "For the Son of man shall come in the glory of his Father with his angels; and then he shall reward every man according to his works. Verily I say unto you, There shall be some standing here, which shall not taste of death, till they see the Son of man coming in his Kingdom." Matthew 16:27-28). That John explicitly understood this teaching is made clear, by the Prophet Joseph Smith, in a remarkable revelation that consists of a translated version of the record made on parchment by John and hidden up by himself. "And the Lord said unto me: John, my beloved, what desirest thou? For if you shall ask what you will, it shall be granted unto you. And I said unto him: Lord, give unto me power over death, that I may live and bring souls unto thee. And the Lord said unto me: Verily, verily, I say unto thee, because thou desirest this thou shalt tarry until I come in my glory, and shalt prophesy before nations, kindreds, tongues and people. And for this cause the Lord said unto Peter: If I will that he tarry till I come, what is that to thee?" (Doctrine and Covenants 7:1-4; compare John 21:20-23). There can scarcely be any mistake that the apostles in the Primitive Church became clearly acquainted with the true doctrine of Christ's Second Coming. If there is any doubt on this point, the Prophet Joseph Smith banished it when he revised by the spirit of revelation the twenty-fourth chapter of Matthew, commencing with the last verse of the Twenty-third chapter, King James' Version. Part of this scripture refers to the coming destruction of the temple and the troubles of the Jews; those parts dealing with the Second Coming are easily picked out. "Then understood his disciples that he should come again on the earth, after that he was glorified and crowned on the right hand of God. . . . But of that day, and hour, no one knoweth; no, not the angels of God in heaven, but my Father only. But as it was in the days of Noah, so it shall be also at the coming of the Son of Man; for it shall be with them, as it was in the days which were before the flood; for until the day that Noah entered into the ark they were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage; and knew not until the flood came, and took them all away; so shall also the coming of the Son of Man be." (Pearl of Great Price, An Extract from a Translation of the Bible, 1:40-43; see also Matthew 24:3-6, 14, 29-30).

6. Some Members of the Primitive Church Clung to Belief in the Quick Return of Christ.—However, in spite of the clear understanding of the doctrine on the part of the apostles, many lay members of the Primitive Church still retained the mistaken impression that the Christ was soon to come again. This is borne out by the fact that the Apostle Paul had to write a letter to the Thessalonians expressly correcting their notions respecting the quick return of the Christ. (2 Thessalonians 2:1-3) In spite of Paul's clear instruction when he was with them, they misunderstood. (2 Thessalonians 2:5) It is likely that at the present time many people misunderstand the doctrine, either because of partially corrupted texts of the Bible, or because some of the New Testament writers, who clearly understood the teaching themselves, have not made themselves sufficiently clear in writing about it.

In this dispensation the Lord has left no doubt in the minds of those who have His Spirit, that His coming is nigh, even at the door.

Questions

1. How do you account for the great indifference of the world today to many sublime truths of the gospel?

2. Can you see wisdom in the Lord's refusal to make known the exact time of His Second Advent?

3. How would you answer those who say a correct understanding of the doctrine of the Second Advent can make no difference in our lives one way or another?

Problems

Treat only those that time and circumstances will permit.

1. Carefully study the Book of Mormon teaching respecting the Second Coming and summarize your findings.

2. Summarize the distinctive contributions of the Doctrine and Covenants to our knowledge of the Second Advent. Which of these are especially hard for the world to receive?

3. Find some contributions of interest made by the Pearl of Great Price to the subject of our lesson.

4. Bring to the class some interesting authoritative statements made by various leaders of the Church that deal with the doctrine of the Second Advent.

articles.title: The Poise of Jesus
articles.text:
Teacher's Topic
LESSON 8

THE POISE OF JESUS

"I am the way, the truth, and the life."—John 14:6.

"Every virtue, when pushed beyond its appointed limit, becomes a vice, and every grace, when overdeveloped, becomes a defect and disfiguration."—Jefferson (C. E.).

POISE is the fine balance of one's faculties, which keeps a virtue within its appointed limits.

In the character of Jesus we find perfect balance. He possessed all the virtues, yet none were overgrown. One writer says: "He was enthusiastic, blazing with enthusiasm, but he never became fanatical. He was emotional; men could feel the throbbing of his heart; but he never became hysterical. He was imaginative, full of poetry and music, throwing upon everything he touched a light that never was on land or sea, the inspiration and the poet's dream—but he was never flighty. He was practical, hardheaded, matter-of-fact, but he was never prosaic, never dull. His life always had in it the glamour of romance. He was courageous, but never reckless; prudent, but never a coward; unique but not eccentric; sympathetic but never sentimental. Great streams of sympathy flowed from his tender heart toward those who needed sympathy, but at the same time streams of lava flowed from the same heart to scorch and overwhelm the workers of iniquity. He was pious, but there is not a trace about him of sanctimoniousness. . . . He was religious, the most profoundly religious man that ever turned his face toward God, but never once did he slip into superstition."—C. E. Jefferson.

Because he was so well rounded, so well balanced, he showed in his conduct unrivaled poise. The intellectuals of his time tried to catch him, or trip him, but his answers were so wise they were vanquished. Satan tempted him to use his power for his own glory, but Jesus never did. His enemies heaped upon him all manner of abuse, but he never said anything unmanly in return. He stood in the midst of the strife and storms, which bent or swayed other men, but he remained unmoved and unmovable. Because he possessed all the higher qualities of character in such balance he appealed to all types of men down through the centuries. The intellectuals go to him for philosophy, for guidance; the painter, the poet, the musician, for inspiration; men who aspire to raise humanity to higher levels go to him humbly because he points the way; those in the midst of strife; those in trouble and sorrow find in him comfort and solace.

He is to men today what he was to men of all centuries since he lived upon the earth, the one perfect character. Only now there is perhaps a greater appreciation of Him and His teachings. He is our pattern and while we can never approach him in perfection of character we can obtain a fuller and more abundant life by following as closely as possible his example.

Discussion

In what way can poise be developed?

Have you known of anyone who changed in character through conversion to the teachings of Jesus?

Give incidents from the New Testament illustrating the poise of Jesus.

References

New Testament.
The Character of Jesus, by Charles Edward Jefferson.

articles.title: The Romance of an Old Playhouse
articles.text:
Literature
LESSON 8

THE ROMANCE OF AN OLD PLAYHOUSE

SIXTY years after the dedication of the Salt Lake Theatre, Henry Miller, in a curtain speech on the stage of the Old Playhouse, referred to it as 'A Cathedral in the Desert.' And surely it has been as a cathedral to the people. Its doors have always been open to all parties and creeds. In the tense religious and political strife which cursed the community, more particularly in the early days, the Theatre was the one common ground upon which the questions of the hour were thrashed out. Though the place has always been controlled by the "Mormon" people, yet its use was never denied to those of different beliefs even though they were violent in their antagonisms. And so it was generally loved by all classes. This neutrality won their respect. The Theatre took on the aspect of a community home and has been so regarded ever since.

The author continues: "Upon its completion the fame of the Theatre spread far and wide. Its comfortable, dignified auditorium, its appointments, stage equipment, decorative art, and marvelous acoustics—all these characteristics marked it as a playhouse of class and distinction. And it was worthy of all that was said of it, for in few cities, even the largest, could be found a playhouse so perfectly furnished. An excavator in Italy recently dug up a plan of what was considered the perfect theatre acoustically; but really to find the most perfect acoustics, they needn't have gone so far, for this feature could have been found in the theatre built by Brigham Young in the Rocky Mountains in 1862."

Those who have had the supreme pleasure of seeing and hearing plays in the Old Playhouse will always remember its beauty as well as its acoustical properties. The writer of these lessons was young when he first began going to plays in the Salt Lake Theatre and though he was usually in the top tier of the topmost "heaven," he still remembers that he could hear and see.

He also remembers going with George D. Pyper under the Old Playhouse to get a glimpse of how it was constructed. Its sturdy foundations and its stout posts of native pine logs impressed him greatly.

That the theatre was not merely a cradle for the drama, but for all of the arts is indicated by Mr. Pyper in his chapter which he calls "Echoes From the Orchestra Pit." In those days, plays invariably had their music between acts and before the play started if not in the piece itself.

Here again the mission fields contributed the geniuses who were needed to make the arts flourish in the desert. The first of these talented men was Professor Charles J. Thomas, who assembled an orchestra of twenty pieces at the opening of the Old Playhouse. From that time on, almost without a break, a fine orchestra could be heard whenever a play was presented.

Professor Thomas was converted in 1851. Falling ill, his life was despaired of, when through the administrations of the elders he was healed. This had a profound effect upon him and his entire family. As a direct result all joined the Church and Professor Thomas came to Utah where a great work awaited him.

Not only did Professor Thomas lead the orchestra, he composed and arranged much of the music which was played. That his efforts were appreciated is indicated by the fact that the old bills frequently featured the music. For instance, "for 'The Charcoal Burner,' produced Saturday, January 10, 1863, the play bill announces 'Beautiful new and appropriate music, composed and arranged expressly for this drama by Prof. C. J. Thomas.'"

Professor Thomas was succeeded as orchestra leader by George Edward Percy Careless, also an English convert. Professor Careless is one of the great geniuses to be gathered by the Gospel net. He could have made much more money and lasting fame elsewhere, but he chose to remain with the people of his choice. Many of our best hymns were written by this man.

Mr. Pyper relates many anecdotes of this gifted musician whose heart was in his work.

Other men who led the orchestra are: F. F. Besley, Willard Weihe, Arthur Shepherd, Fred Midgley, Squire Coop, and Arthur Freber.

The first Utahn ever to write an overture was William C. Clive, a well known musician and first violinist of the orchestra. His number, "The Golden Crest," was played first in the Salt Lake Theatre.

Mention has already been made of the painting. The Theatre, itself, was a challenge to the artist. It was beautifully finished in harmonious colors that made an evening in its spacious auditorium an event worthy of being held as a sacred memory. Its great front curtain and its drops were also inspirational to those who had the privilege of seeing a play on its stage. Naturally, it was a constant incentive to the dancer and the musician, vocal or instrumental, who looked upon an appearance in the Theatre as being an important step on the way toward recognition.

One of the most popular of the early-day actresses to come to the theatre was Julia Dean Hayne. Her opening performance as a member of the Potter Company occurred August 11, 1865 when she appeared in "Camille." She proved tremendously popular and remained at the Theatre for more than a year, dying soon after leaving Salt Lake City. The author has included in his book, mention of a huge sled which President Young caused to be built and named the Julia Dean.

President Young's great interest in the drama and the Old Playhouse is presented no more forcibly anywhere than in the chapter where attention is called to the fact that he actually called at least a few young people on missions to act, though such calls were rarely necessary because all who had the least ability were eager, beyond measure, to play behind the footlights of the West's great playhouse.

Miss Nellie Colebrook was one of the first called to act in the Old Playhouse. The letter of call follows:

"Dear Brother and Sister Colebrook:
"Would you allow your daughter Nellie to act upon the stage? It would very much please me.
Your Brother,
Brigham Young."

The author follows with this statement:

"A published biographical sketch of Miss Colebrook's life describes her as a very diffident girl, but very queenly, and adds: 'How had he, the President of the Church, divined all the secret aspirations and ambitions of that reserved and shy soul! Who had heard from the sensitive lips one word of all that had burned in her heart!' To Nellie those questions always remained unanswered!"

Sara Alexander was another girl who was called upon a "Mission" to act.

"President Young asked her one day:
"'Sara, why don't you join the company?'
"'I don't want to,' she replied.
"'But I want you to!' said the President. And Sara complied and made an instantaneous hit."

Both of these girls became unusually popular as actresses.

Companies came and went. The Old Playhouse continued to be first in the hearts of the Saints who packed it whenever something good was presented. Many of the leading actors and actresses remained at the Theatre for weeks, playing different plays until they were well known and many of them were deeply loved.

President Young preferred comedies. He said: "If I had my way I would never have a tragedy played on these boards. There is enough tragedy in everyday life, and we ought to have amusement when we come here." However, he was not very dictatorial in these matters.

As early as 1869 the first operas were presented in the Old Playhouse, the first company being the Howson Opera Company. Their season was from June 1 to June 19. Among the operas they presented were "Il Trovatore" and the "Grand Duchess."

During that year, 1869, the Old Playhouse was open 283 nights, an indication that the dramatic presentations brought to Salt Lake City were popular with the people.

The coming of Maude Adams to the Old Playhouse is considered by the author a high spot in its history. He says: "As bluebirds in the spring suddenly arrive in our gardens as harbingers of happiness and good luck, Maude Adams, when only a birdling, fluttered into the theatre in the spring of its existence and became a part of its romance, bringing new faith and confidence in the wholesomeness and worthwhileness of the stage."

Mr. Pyper, as all others who knew her, adored Maude Adams and looked upon her as being among the greatest of the great.

In a pleasing reminiscent mood, the author wrote his chapters 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, and 23 as he traced the intimate history of the building, that he loved, and the artistic expression for which it stood. Those chapters are rich in intimate glimpses of theatre life.

Suggestions for Study

1. It would be well to assign the chapter—"The Advent of Maude Adams—Her Mother's Story"—to some capable person and have her give it rather in full.

2. The anecdotes concerning Brigham Young in chapter 23 could also be given rather fully.

3. Careless' and Thomas' songs could be sung to correlate with this lesson. These may be found in the "Sons of Zion" or the "Deseret Sunday School Song Book."

4. Enumerate ways in which the Old Playhouse encouraged the arts.

5. Give evidences that President Young was strongly in favor of dramatic productions.

6. Where the plays are available, excerpts from some of these first plays could be presented in class.

articles.title: Spiritual Qualities in the Church Beautification Program
articles.text:
Social Service
LESSON 8

SPIRITUAL QUALITIES IN THE CHURCH BEAUTIFICATION PROGRAM

IN THE Church Beautification Program there is one phase that supersedes all tangible evidence of what is grand in architecture, of what nears perfection in the art of sculpture and in painting, or of any surroundings that may be brought into a state of loveliness to enhance a building—it is the condition of spiritual beauty through which our church was organized.

There is one note which should be sounded in order to sustain that kind of beauty in the hearts of those who are fortunate enough to live in an age when the beautification program can be launched—the humility of remembrance.

Time has done much for the Latter-day Saints; it has given the strength and the power to project this program. But time must not be allowed to diminish the grandeur of the spirit which outshone the poignantly humble beginnings of the "Mormons" in a material way.

To call this fact to the attention of the reader is the purpose of this lesson.

THE Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints came to life through beauty. The implicit faith of the lad, Joseph Smith, prompted him to seek guidance through prayer. On the morning of a clear day in spring, for the first time in his life, he sought to pray vocally. "Never before had I made such an attempt," the young boy said.

But his unfaltering belief in the promise of the Scriptures resulted

articles.title: Lesson Department - Theology and Testimony - Lesson 1: The Mediterranean World in the Days of the Apostles
articles.text:
Lesson Department
Theology and Testimony
Lesson 1
THE MEDITERRANEAN WORLD IN THE DAYS OF THE APOSTLES
Helpful References
H. E. Dana, The New Testament World, pp. 126-162. (Second Ed., Pioneer Publishing Co., Fort Worth, Texas. 1928.) Dana's book will be found the best single practical reference for this and the following lesson. This book is now out of print. Some second-hand copies may possibly be obtained at Blessing Book Stores, Inc., Chicago, Ill.
B. W. Robinson, The Life of Paul, pp. 5-24. (University of Chicago Press, Chicago, Ill. 1918.) Note the list of supplementary readings on p. 24. If you have access to a library some of the books mentioned will probably be available.
J. P. Smyth, The Story of St. Paul's Life and Letters, pp. 8-14. (James Pott & Co., New York. 1921.)
T. G. Tucker, Life in the Roman World of Nero and St. Paul, pp. 16-29, 238-259. (The Macmillan Co., New York. 1911.) A very readable work by a great classical scholar. Valuable to those persons who wish an intimate view of Roman life in the days of the apostles. Consult good library.
The World into Which the Apostles Came.—The apostles of Christ came into a world already old. Many civilizations had risen, flourished, decayed and fallen around the Mediterranean Sea long before they appeared on the scene. The great nations of Egypt, Babylonia, Assyria and Persia had played their parts on the world's stage and had departed. The influence of Greece was still wonderfully felt in the days of the apostles, but her glory had departed as a nation. Rome was now the great world power. All of the civilized peoples of northern Africa, western Asia and southern Europe were under the political sway of the Romans.
Travel by Land in the Roman Empire.—The Roman Empire has been variously estimated as containing from fifty to seventy-five millions of peoples of many nationalities, speaking many different tongues and possessing varied customs. These people were of differing degrees of culture and advancement. It is a remarkable commentary on the Romans that they were bringing the world to a state of peace and order. They built wonderful roads, fostered commerce and travel, made intercourse safe and easy between most parts of the empire, often protected the Christian missionaries from the fury of mobs. The traveler would find milestones in the shape of short pillars near the highway on which he traveled. Guidebooks and charts were common. They gave all information necessary such as distances, stopping-places, inns, and even told about the sights most worth seeing while on the journey. Professional guides were available in most places of special interest such as Egypt, Syria and Greece. Travelers rode on asses, mules and horses or took their ease in two or four-wheeled vehicles, depending on their circumstances. According to Professor Tucker "the rich man, traveling, as he necessarily would, with a train of servants and with full appliances for his comfort, would journey in a coach, painted and gilded, cushioned and curtained, drawn by a team showily caparisoned with rich harness and coloured cloths." It was not unusual for people to travel seventy to one hundred miles a day. Special couriers often traveled one hundred and fifty miles in one day.
Travel By Sea.—The amount of travel by sea was remarkable. Vessels were often of a good size, ranging from 500 tons up to 1100 or more. In dimensions they were often 200 feet long and 50 broad. Paul the Apostle speaks of his ship carrying 276 passengers (Acts 27: 37) and Josephus mentions a ship on which he traveled that carried 600 persons. Certain war vessels could carry as high as a thousand soldiers. Freight of from 250 to 1,500 tons was carried by vessels plying the seas. Regular sailing lines were established. The ancient apostles would have no trouble making their way about the Mediterranean world by water. Shipwrecks were not uncommon, as they always were in the days of sailing vessels. But the relative ease of travel was a great advantage in spreading the Gospel.
An Intimate View of Roman Life.—The Roman world of that day had its morning newspapers, its trade unions, its loan sharks, its war-profiteers, its Socialistic agitators, its complaint about the decay of the middle classes and the falling birthrate, its crowded cities and depopulated countryside, its fully organized credit and banking systems. It has been pointed out that practically every comfortable villa of the first century A. D. had a hot-air heating system. It is a well-known fact that their vices were nearly the same as in our own age. They were gambling, luxury, and a mad rush for wealth, together with appalling sexual immorality and a degrading idleness among the very rich and the very poor. They had their professional athletes and actors who earned fabulous sums, they had their comic artists and cartoonists, they had their horse-races and betting on horses, they had public libraries in every moderately large town (the donor's names were duly recorded), and their book, art and curio-collectors. Universities were in existence in certain key cities that attracted students from all parts of the Roman world. There were itinerant lecturers aplenty and even "honorary" degrees were awarded. The dictation of letters was nearly as common in the first century as today and at least three different systems of shorthand were in vogue.
Social Revolution in the First Century.—The first century was one of social revolution. The Roman nobles had lost their wealth and prestige and the grumbling of the "common people" was bringing about a social upheaval. Scarcely two generations after Christ's death hundreds of thousands of unemployed burst into Rome and surrounded the imperial palace howling for bread.
Roman Princes and Freedmen.—Roman princes and senators were forbidden by law to increase their fortunes by commerce. Honest toil was despised by them. The noble class were often obliged by reason of extravagant tastes and the cost of holding public office to promise freedom to their slaves in return for carrying on financial plans more energetically. Many of these freedmen became wealthy captains of industry. Their gorgeous banquets and excessive orgies in which they displayed their vulgarity and ignorance won them the ill-will of the cultured poor and the titled nobility.
Poverty Widespread in the Roman World.—Most of the freedmen with whom the early Christians worked were not wealthy. The proportion of poor to rich was much higher in the days of the apostles than it is today. Poverty was more widespread than now, and it has been pointed out that for the first time in history the poor man had become dangerous.
Corporations and Monopolies.—Corporations and monopolies had such control of foodstuffs that some fifty years before Christ nearly 300,000 persons had to be fed by the State in order to survive. Politicians courted the favor of citizens by promising gifts of foodstuffs and free shows.
Religious Tolerance of the Romans.—With all their failings the Romans were tolerant for the most part in matters of religion. They recognized the value of religion and were wise enough not to interfere with it in the countries they conquered, provided their own civil requirements were observed.
Roman Rule in the Provinces.—The Romans were quite generally wise and generous in their administration of conquered states. They did not interfere with laws and customs already established so far as these were compatible with the rule of Rome. Some provinces were governed by vassal kings, who were subject to Rome and paid a certain tribute. The Herods of the New Testament are good examples of this custom. Generally a Roman governor was appointed directly from Rome by either the Senate or the Emperor. These governors were charged with keeping the province in subjection to Rome, collecting taxes, and administering justice wherever a matter concerned Roman law. Local courts tried violators of local laws. A defendant might appeal directly to Rome if he was a Roman citizen. It was under this provision that Paul appealed to Caesar.
Greece the Roman Schoolmaster.—In the days of the apostles Greece had long been a province of Rome. But the Romans recognized that the Greeks were their intellectual and artistic superiors. The result was that the Greeks became the schoolmasters to Rome. "The entire first century world went to school to Hellas—and the world has not yet quit going to school to Hellas." It is no exaggeration to say, "Greece captive led enthralled her captor." The Greeks contributed mightily not only to Rome but to our own western culture as well. What would philosophy, art, literature and science have been without the clear-thinking Greeks?
Greek Language and Literature in the Roman World.—When Alexander the Great went forth to conquer, the Greek language was planted in most of the countries he subdued. It became the chief medium of expression in the eastern Mediterranean basin. Rich in vocabulary and capable of expressing accurately subtle distinctions in thought it became a popular tongue. When the Romans conquered Greece they carried the Greek language back with them to the West. It became the chief literary medium and was a commonly spoken tongue even in imperial Rome. When the apostles and other Christian missionaries carried the Gospel to the West they could make themselves understood almost anywhere they chose to go because of their knowledge of Greek. The spread of this richly expressive language would seem almost providential. Greek was one of the chief languages in Palestine and it appears almost certain that Christ and most of his apostles knew something about it even though their ordinary means of expression was Aramaic. At any rate the Gospel of John takes it for granted that the knowledge of Greek was common in Galilee. Pilate, Felix, and Festus, the representatives of the Roman government, seem to have used it, for the Palestinian inscriptions referring to the Roman Emperors, when of a non-military nature, were written mostly in Greek. Josephus tells us that it was only in the army that the Latin language ruled supreme.
The Greeks had a wonderful literary tradition. The beauty of Greek literature has had, and still has, great influence upon the literature of even our own day. It should not surprise us, therefore, to find that Greek literature was supreme in the Roman world. Cultivated minds were familiar with Homer's Iliad and Odyssey as a matter of course, and the poems of such men as Theognis, Pindar, Bacchylides, Alcaeus, Sappho and Simonides, as well as the historical prose productions of Thucydides, Herodotus and Xenophon were widely read; Greek works on rhetoric and oratory would also be well known. Among good thinkers the works of Plato, Aristotle, Anaximander, Pythagoras and other Greek philosophers would be much sought after and studied. We cannot help but feel that the ancient apostles would make themselves somewhat acquainted with the literature and thought of their day. Certainly the apostle Paul could hold the attention of cultured Athenians so long as he did not offend by speaking on the doctrine of the resurrection. There is some evidence from the New Testament that Paul was familiar with certain Greek writers (Acts 17:28; 1 Cor. 15:33; Tit. 1:12.)
From what we have already pointed out it will be seen how important it is to know something of the world in which the apostles moved if we are to understand their work and ministry.
Questions and Problems
(Deal only with as many as time will permit.)
1. In what ways was the Roman government an aid to the spread of the Gospel in the days of the apostles?
2. Compare methods of travel in the age of the apostles with those in vogue among our pioneer forefathers.
3. Compare Roman life with that in our own age.
4. Can you make some interesting comparisons between the social revolution of the First Century and that in our country in the past few years?
5. Name as many elements in our civilization as you can for which we are indebted to the Greeks.
6. What problems would confront the ancient apostles due to the widespread poverty?

articles.title: Visiting Teacher Department - Messages to the Home - No. 1—Faith
articles.text:
Visiting Teacher Department
MESSAGES TO THE HOME
No. 1.—FAITH
"Jesus answered and said unto them, Verily I say unto you, If ye have faith, and doubt not, ye shall not only do this which is done to the fig tree, but, also if ye shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed, and be thou cast into the sea; it shall be done."—Matt. 21:21.
MENTAL health, powerful character and desirable personality are sought by every individual. Faith creates a healthy emotional attitude and is a source of true greatness in attaining these. One must have faith in his own ability, faith in the value of his task, faith in his fellowman and faith in a supreme power. The perfect personality has these faiths.
Faith as Jesus taught it can be applied to the production of healthy personalities. He taught an intelligent faith in self which is aware of danger and adjusts to it, but without fear. He taught a faith in fellowman which does not close its eyes to faults but recognizes the highest potentialities. He taught a faith in God—the Eternal Father.
Peter desired to do the things the Master did. During the time that he had faith in the Master and was confident, he possessed the power to do the things which the Master did, even to overcoming the law of gravity and walking on the water. When doubt entered his mind, faith departed and with it that power which ever accompanies faith was lost, and Peter sank.
While we have confidence in self and faith in the Master we have power to rise above the sea of earthly troubles; our characters are strong, our personalities healthy.
Each person has within himself the possibility of developing this power of faith. It is a plant of slow growth. The seed must be planted consciously and nurtured carefully if we are to expect the tree to attain maturity and bear fruit.
1. Give illustrations of faith in self as taught by Jesus.
2. Give illustrations of how faith in God has increased power.
3. Explain the relationship of faith to personality.
Visiting Teachers are to call attention to Theology, Literary and Social Service lessons, also Work and Business programs as planned by your ward.

articles.title: Literature - The Advance of the Novel - Lesson 1: The Beginnings of the English Novel
articles.text:
Literature
THE ADVANCE OF THE NOVEL
Lesson 1
THE BEGINNINGS OF THE ENGLISH NOVEL
DEFINITIONS OF A NOVEL
(Gathered by Prof. J. M. Jensen)
"A novel is an interpretation of human life by means of fictitious narrative in prose." — Ernest A. Baker, English writer.
"The novel is a picture of real life and manners, and of the times of which it is written. It gives a familiar relation of such things as pass every day before our eyes, such as may happen to our friend or to ourselves; and the perfection of it is to represent every scene in so easy and natural a manner and to make it appear so probable as to deceive into a persuasion (at least while we are reading) that all is real, until we are affected by the joys or distresses of the persons in the story as if they were our own."—Clara Reever, early English novelist.
"A novel is a record of emotion." —Francis Hovey Stoddard, Professor at New York University.
"The novel may be thought of as a prose fiction, regularly over forty thousand words long, so planned that characters, dialogue, and setting will fuse with all the skill the author can muster in the telling."—Grant C. Knight, University of Kentucky.
During this and the lessons which are to follow, a study will be made of the beginning and advance of the novel. Nearly everybody reads more or less and in our day. Among the choicest of our reading materials are the novels which have poured and which are still pouring from our printing presses. If that is the case, it is well that we should learn what we can about the beginning and the development of this form of creative writing.
Perhaps it would be well to speak further of definitions, even though they are always difficult and we can never say exactly what we mean.
What is a novel, then?
We shall make no attempt to draw fine distinctions, but will suggest distinguishing features of this favorite form of literature.
In the first place, a novel is a long story dealing usually with situations, characters, and complications. Novels vary in length, but we usually think of them, as was indicated in one of Professor Jensen's definitions, as being forty or fifty thousand up to several hundred thousand words in length. If stories are shorter than forty thousand they are usually referred to as short novels or novelettes.
For the reason that all long stories are not novels it may be well to limit our definition still further. Two broad classes into which we may divide longer works of fiction are romances and novels. We must not forget, however, that the line between these two forms is so indistinct and so indefinite that often it fades away entirely and we find one good critic calling a story a novel at the same time that another equally good is calling it a romance. The extremes in both fields, however, are rather easily recognized.
As I remember, it was Fred Lewis Pattee who gave voice to this statement which may be helpful: The romancer sits down to write what he has to tell; the novelist, to write what he thinks or has to say.
William Lyon Phelps, in our text, says upon this subject: "It is somewhat unfortunate, in discussing the history of English prose fiction, that we cannot make a sharp distinction between the words 'romance' and 'novel.' We ought to mean by 'romance' a story where the chief interest lies, not in the characters, but in the events; as, for example, Quentin Durward. By the word 'novel' we should denote a story where the principal stress falls, not in the succession of incidents, but on the development of the characters; an excellent illustration would be The Mill On The Floss."
Professor Phelps, however, goes on to point out the difficulty in making such a distinction as he suggests by stating that many a man has fused the two forms so well that it would be improper to label his product as either. "Henry Esmond," Professor Phelps states, "if a secret ballot could be taken, might possibly be voted the greatest work of fiction in the English language . . ." and yet he intimates that Henry Esmond is hardly a romance and hardly a novel either.
The reader will remember, then, that the term "novel" is a rather loose one designating long prose fiction in which the emphasis is on the character development and the interrelations of the characters rather than on the incidents. If one would sit down and write the story of his own life or that of his father or mother as it unfolded, emphasizing those things which directed character growth, he probably would have a novel when he finished. If he should sit down and write the same story emphasizing what the characters did rather than what they were, he would most likely have a romance. It must not be forgotten that the distinction, in any case, is difficult and in some cases impossible.
There are things of which we can be certain, however. If one is to lay claim to artistic culture and is to enjoy his priceless literary heritage, one must know something of the world's great literature and especially of his own. Americans, of course, are rightful heirs to the English tradition in literature.
The English novel, as we think of it today, had its beginning back in the beginning of the eighteenth century. It was really a tale of adventure, but because of the author's method it might well be called a novel as it is called by no less a person than Professor Phelps and by many other critics.
"The first English novel is still one of the most popular—Robinson Crusoe, by Daniel Defoe, published in 1719," says Phelps.
This long story of the fortunes of a rather incorrigible young man who ran away from home and went to sea, is the forerunner of many other stories which followed a little later.
To Samuel Richardson, another Englishman and contemporary of Defoe, goes the distinction of having written the first analytical or psychological novel. Richardson wrote as his first book a long novel called Pamela, a tale written entirely in the form of letters. This first book of his was followed by volume two of the same story and by other well-known novels such as Clarissa Harlowe and Sir Charles Grandison.
Henry Fielding, Tobias George Smollett, and Laurence Sterne were three other Englishmen living about the same time as Defoe and Richardson, who set the English novel well on its way in three great books known to all casual students of English literature: Tom Jones, Roderick Random, and Life and Opinions of Tristram Shandy.
"Prose fiction dates well before the eighteenth century," says Abel Chevalley in his book The Modern English Novel, "but the English novel goes back no further than that century. Before Defoe, all writing comprised under this name seems to have been imported from abroad. It was not until after Defoe, when Richardson and Fielding had published their books, that England, having patented her goods—if one may so express it—began to export and to conquer; and in her turn distributed over the world the new literary type that she had developed."
Daniel Defoe was born in London, the son of a butcher, in 1661. His father's name was Foe, but Daniel added the prefix De to give the name a little better sound. He is said to have been one of England's most prolific writers, being given credit for being England's first journalist in the modern sense and the author of the interview and the special editorial.

articles.title: Literature - The Advance of the Novel - Lesson 1 (continued)
articles.text:
In 1702 Defoe published an article which caused him to be placed in the pillory and later in Newgate prison where he associated with criminals and adventurers who had been in all parts of the world. It is thought that from them he gathered much of the material which he later placed in his novels which he wrote with great rapidity even though he was more than forty-five years of age when he began upon them. He died in 1731, "a real or fancied fugitive from the public."
The English novel with Defoe got started upon its glorious trail toward the stars. According to Professor Phelps, if Defoe had never written his masterpiece, Robinson Crusoe, he would still be the first novelist among English people. Pamela, by Richardson, is often referred to as the first novel, but as has been inferred, it was so named by those who make a sharp division between romance and the realistic novel. In this course, we are not doing that; we are following more or less the easy trail marked out by Professor Phelps.
Suggestions
1. Read chapters I and II, "The Advance of the English Novel," by William Lyon Phelps. Read also, if it should happen to be available, Development of the English Novel, by W. L. Cross, (Macmillan), or any other such book that may be at hand.
2. Give some definitions of a novel. It may be that you can think of a better one than any given here.
3. Comment upon the first sentence found in chapter two of the Advance of the English Novel: "Queen Anne brought prose fiction from heaven or hell to earth, and gave us the novel." The men of Queen Anne were Defoe, Richardson, Smollett, etc.
4. Perhaps class members could be assigned the pleasant task of presenting briefly the lives of two of our first novelists:
(1) Defoe. (2) Richardson.
Sketches could be found in any library and in some school texts and encyclopedias.

articles.title: Social Service - Lesson 1: Habit Formation and Revision
articles.text:
Social Service
Lesson 1
HABIT FORMATION AND REVISION
I. The Lesson in a Nutshell. A large part of each day's activities consists of habitual acts which have been repeated in a certain way so often that they "carry themselves along" with very little conscious direction. Habit is a great principle of economy: it saves conscious effort for those tasks which require deliberation. Habits are of many kinds: (1) social habits, (2) intellectual habits, (3) emotional habits, (4) nervous habits, (5) physiological habits, and (6) motor habits or skills.
Habits grow out of incentives or needs. To build a new habit or to break an old one is possible only by revising our incentives and needs, then by analysis of our efforts in the new direction, and finally by enlightened practice. The lesson is concluded with a practical guide for the formation of new habits and breaking of old ones.

short.text:
Relief Society Conference
April 1 and 2, 1938
By Julia A. F. Lund, General Secretary

THE Annual Conference of the Relief Society was held April 1 and 2, 1938, in Salt Lake City, Utah. President Louise Y. Robison presided. The following sessions were held: Officers' Meeting, Social Welfare Department, Work and Business Department, Relief Society Magazine Department, Secretaries' Department, Music Department. Two general sessions were held in the Tabernacle. The reception for the Singing Mothers, the one for the State Officers, and the President's Breakfast were delightful events.

The attendance was very gratifying, and included enthusiastic workers from 116 stakes and 12 missions. The following is the attendance record at the Officers' Meeting held in the Assembly Hall, Friday morning, April 1, at 10 o'clock: Mission Presidents, 12; Stake Presidents, 108; Counselors, 135; Secretaries, 67; Board Members, 404; Total, 726. The ushers were from the Salt Lake and Liberty State Relief Societies.

Among the outstanding features of the Conference were the magnificent music, and the launching of the Membership Campaign. In connection with this, special attention must be called to the membership song, "One Hundred Thousand Strong," composed by Beatrice F. Stevens of the General Board of Relief Society. The "Rallying Song" was introduced by a double trio from the Ensign Stake, directed by Ruth Jensen Clawson, and a talented group representative of Relief Society members with Jean H. Wessman as accompanist. Others whose talents in music added much to the meetings were Lily Priestly's organ prelude, Inez Robinson Preece's quartet, and the beautiful solo by Eliza Bringhurst. The combined choruses of Relief Society Singing Mothers, directed by Charlotte O. Sackett, with Alta B. Cassity and Frank Asper as accompanists, made Relief Society history.

OFFICERS' MEETING
GREETINGS AND OFFICIAL INSTRUCTIONS
By President Louise Y. Robison

MEETING with the devoted leaders of Relief Society is a continued source of joy and inspiration.

REFERRING to the ruling on donations received by Visiting Teachers, we feel more strongly than ever that all donations collected by Visiting Teachers belong in the Charity Fund. If members of Relief Society or others wish to contribute to the General Fund, we shall be most grateful to them if they will give or send such contributions to the President or Secretary of the Relief Society.

WE would like to emphasize the request that officers refrain from using Relief Society for advertising purposes. The fine project for better methods of laundering clothes brought many offers from soap manufacturers for demonstration in Relief Society. We are quite opposed to having any commercial article featured in our meetings.

WE have received many compliments from the General Authorities of the Church, morticians, and from Relief Society Presidents on our suggestion that funeral flowers remain undisturbed until after the mourners have gone from the building. We were advised to take this action by members of the General Authorities.

IN a few of our stakes, a charge is made for luncheon served on Work and Business day. We hope you will find some other way of obtaining finances. Many of our sisters would be happy to pay for their luncheon, but in each ward there are fine, faithful members who cannot afford this expense and who will not feel comfortable in attending these meetings.

THERE are occasionally Ward Presidents who do not work in harmony with their Stake President. They do not attend Union meetings and feel that they need no advice in the conduct of the Relief Society work. This is a serious condition. There must be complete harmony between stake and ward officers or the work of Relief Society suffers. Before any woman accepts an office in any department of Relief Society she should know what the requirements are and be willing to live up to them. Unless ward officers give loyal support to stake officers, they cannot expect loyalty from their own group. Remember always that the spirit of the Lord is with us only when there is harmony and a desire to cooperate for the good of the whole group.

WE have had a wonderful response to the hurried request sent from our office a week ago for a report on what the Relief Society had done for Church Security. The Presiding Bishopric asked us to get the report for the First Presidency to use at Conference. Within a week, ninety-nine stakes and missions had reported. Although the Southern States Mission is so far away our Mission Mother, Sister Clayson, had her report in the office on time.

IN making the survey for the clothing requirements of our people Relief Society has discovered some families in urgent need at the present time. In a few cases the bishops have requested the Relief Society to supply those needs from their funds. Evidently these bishops are new in the work, as Relief Society has not now, nor ever has had, sufficient funds to care for rent, light, food and clothing. Relief Society offers service. Our funds are to be used in cases of emergency and to supplement the work of the bishop.

THERE are some very fine organizations outside of our Relief Society who are working for the benefit of humanity. We are eager to have our Societies cooperate in spreading useful information but we are opposed to having regular Relief Society class work interfered with or having dues to any other organization collected in our meetings. To the extent that individual members can subscribe to these helpful movements we are in hearty accord, but this is entirely outside of the Relief Society.

WE advise Relief Societies to cooperate with the Agricultural Colleges, with the county agents and demonstrators in any state in which they reside. These trained people have valuable information on the needs of the body and the kinds of food which supply those needs. Deficiency diseases, which result from improper diet, are as great a menace as the contagious diseases. To understand the chemical requirements of the body and to know the foods which supply those needs is of great importance to mothers.

WE are making a change in the Relief Society Magazine Honor Roll awards. Instead of all Relief Societies competing on the same basis, there will be three groups. One group will include all wards and branches with membership up to 49 inclusive. One group will be those Societies whose membership is from 50 to 99 inclusive, and a third group will include those with a membership of 100 or over. We feel this is more just.

Presidents will please appoint a member of the Board to be responsible for the activities of ward Magazine Agents. We have had heartbreaking experiences when some of the ward Agents, who have worked very hard to get subscriptions, have sent their reports to the stakes and the stakes have failed to get the information to headquarters in time for the credits to be awarded.

WE acknowledge with deep appreciation the very interesting publications which come to us from the missions. One recently reached us from Sister Nibley of the Northwestern States Mission with some very fine reports. The Northern States Mission sends them to us frequently. They are most helpful. Sister May Green Hinckley of the Northern States Mission gave us the very fine idea of placing useful Relief Society information on the back of our membership cards. We shall adopt this suggestion when we print other cards.

May the Lord bless you with vision to see the needs, and health to carry forward the work in a manner pleasing and acceptable to yourselves and to our Heavenly Father.

ANNOUNCEMENT
By President Louise Y. Robison

THE Government has appointed Miss Daisy Duncombe to make a survey of the hard of hearing who are living in Utah. If you know any persons who are hard of hearing, please advise them to communicate with Miss Duncombe, 1259 Sherman Avenue, Salt Lake City, giving information relative to age, sex, and education.

ANNUAL REPORT
Julia A. F. Lund, General Secretary

IT is always a rare opportunity to present a very brief summary of the Annual Report of the Relief Society and to express the deep appreciation the general office feels for the splendid cooperation of the Stakes and Missions.

The reports this year were more perfect than ever before. We are grateful to our fine secretaries.

The following is a brief annual statement for the year 1937. This includes Missions, Stake Boards and Ward organizations.

short.title: Aunt Heartha
short.author: Not clearly visible in the pages shown
short.text:
aniums with Aunt Heartha coaxing them into bud and bloom. Hap, meanwhile, was thinking only of Christy. His Christy!

She glanced up at him with an ingratiating smile. "I had no idea you had so thoroughly remodeled," said she.

Hap opened an adjoining door. "This room is rather small," said he, "but will be convenient for an occasional guest." Christy's face was radiant and sunny as she stepped inside the cozy little sanctum. Inaudibly her heart exclaimed, "Heartha's own little room!"

Closing the door, Hap remarked, "This is an old-fashioned stairway with worn steps, Christy, but none of them creak." Hap grasped her outstretched hand and slowly led her up the narrow passage. With the opening of the loft door came a blue-bending arch of sky.

"A roof garden!" exclaimed Christy. "Whoever would have dreamed of such a luxury!" Turning to Hap she whispered, "Dearest, you did this all for me!" He smiled a slow, meditative smile and gave answer:

"To tell you fact, Christy, I've stayed awake nights thinking this out—a place for you to steal away by yourself to sketch the summer landscape. Here you'll be at liberty to apply yourself to your beloved art unmolested."

Christy's eyes were resting on the fields of snow, and beyond the willow-lined creek and little groups of young oak trees. She seemed dumb with happiness and surprise.

"In early spring," continued Hap, "when the green begins to creep back into the trees and the spaces between show the bonny blue sky"—

A LOUD, clamorous knock at the front door called them below at once.

A hearty old Scotchman stood on the front porch. He was smiting his mittened hands together and stamping the snow from his overshoes. His cheeks were ruddy and his breath came like white vapor.

"It means me the breadth's come alive this mornin'—smoke curlin' from the chimney. Are you young folk movin' in?"

"Not today. Won't you take this rocker, Uncle Alec?"

"Don't want to sit down. I'd rather go below and have a good long look at your stoker. I might watch it half an hour, if you don't mind."

"Go right ahead, Uncle Alec," said Hap as he turned good-naturedly back to his conversation with Christy.

"Maybe you'll want a fireplace in this room. Sort of friendly, wouldn't it be, having a fireplace?"

Christy could see dear old Heartha in the glow of a cheery yule log and her heart exulted at the picture. Deftly she hid it from Hap, who suddenly burst out: "Your presence here seems to brighten the very air, Christy and just think, this home is ours alone—yours and mine. Sometimes it seems as if this whole, big world holds but you and me."

Christy who ordinarily would have responded to these warm, glowing words, seemed actually to shiver. Hap perceived it.

"You aren't cold dear: What's the matter?" he asked with a penetrating look as if trying to probe her inner conscience.

"I've changed my mind, Hap. That's all. This house isn't for me."

Hap drew his lips into a thin, straight line. "What's wrong with you, Christy?"

Her voice lowered to a whisper. "I can't go on."

Hap eyed her closely. Her face looked drained and bleak—utterly colorless—but there was burning pain in her eyes. Never before had he seen Christy like this—his clear-eyed, trusty pal—the one girl in all the world who had no skill in evasion or lies.

"Is it something I've done, Christy?"

"No." In spite of her outward calm, Christy's throat cramped and her voice was near suffocation. "I can't marry you, Hap. That's all."

Hap's face looked haggard. He cleared his throat roughly. It seemed to him in this emotional crisis that Christy wasn't Christy at all. Another girl stood in her place before him. And this girl was unfeeling, hardened, acutely cruel! Worst of all, there was nothing he could do about it. The silent rage that seized him was gruelling to his nerves.

"I'll take the interurban car back to Cedarville," announced Christy.

MECHANICALLY the two of them made their way back to the little blue roadster by the gate. With chill hands, Hap clutched the wheel and they were off.

At the depot Christy forced back unshed tears and nodded goodbye. Hap replied with a fixed, unseeing stare and a frigid bow.

The train pulled out of Riverbend with its customary stutter of start and stop. Soon it gained its usual rhythm. Hap stood watching until it had disappeared behind "Queen's Bluff"—a cliff of colored stone near the turn of the track.

"Queen's Bluff," he ejaculated ironically as he leaped into his car and banged the door. His reckless driving might have cost him a heavy fine had there been a rush of traffic.

Upon entering the door he stumbled over Christy's valise filled with art material. This gave him pause. He reached down and unlocked it. There was every needful tool and brush in perfect order and readiness for a day of sketching.

"She even denied herself that special treat," said Hap aloud. He felt certain that their suffering at parting had been mutual. He sat down and examined the contents of her art-folio. There he found sketches on various themes—a face, a bust, a tree, a distant hill, even a placard bearing a printed quotation, "When half-gods go, the gods arrive."

SOME one was stirring in the basement tinkering with the stoker. Hap listened to big footsteps, and presently Uncle Alec stalked into the room. Uncle Alec could see trouble on Hap's brow and blurted out, "It couldn't by any chance have been a lover's quarrel between you and Christy?"

"We've never quarreled in our lives and we've been seeing each other more than three years," explained Hap.

"Then how come she's gone?"

"As to the reason, Uncle Alec, I'm safe in saying you know as much about it as I do."

Uncle Alec sniffed in a droll, bewildered way as if he might accidentally scent the hidden clue.

"Is it something you might have done, Hap, or is it something you've failed to do?"

"I didn't ask. Is there anything I've left undone, Uncle Alec?"

Uncle Alec pondered, cleared his throat as if to speak, then remained silent.

"Out with it! Come on!" urged Hap.

"Well—there's Heartha. Did you have a good heart-to-heart chat with Christy concernin' Heartha?"

"I never mentioned her name. Not even once.

"I thought maybe you would-a mentioned how nice to have a visit from Heartha once in a while. It would have been handy-like to have said so when you and Christy were lookin' over these rooms together."

At these words there dawned a mysterious awareness in Hap's eyes as he gazed confidently into Uncle Alec's kindly face.

"What do you know about Heartha, Uncle Alec?"

"I only know what I saw with my own eyes and heard with my ears. Wasn't I in the funeral twenty years ago—the double funeral for Christy's parents? Her mother and daddy died within twenty-four hours of one another. I was present at the services.

"Heartha took Christy Clemens, a motherless infant only three weeks old, and did a mother's part by her. You know what my meanin' is when I say she went the second mile. There's a bit of scripture that exhorts one to travel the second mile, do more than's asked. Well, Heartha Wylie went the second mile as a mother. Christy had schoolin', sewin', cookin', music lessons, parties. I bet my bottom dollar she has had more fine clothes in her young life than Heartha's ever owned in all these years."

Hap seemed profoundly touched, meditative, and gravely silent.

"Well my boy, it's how I guess you've heard enough. It's just another story of Ruth cleavin' to Naomi. Christy can't tear away from Heartha, at least not in one sudden jerk. It's like this, Hap, you're young. You're not a mind-reader. It takes the mellowin' touch of time to be able to discern the inside workin's of a woman's mind. Comes like a gift to some few men."

HAP'S eyes were aglow with a strange illumination. He grabbed up his hat and said, "I'm off, Uncle Alec. I'm going to beat that interurban car into Cedarville."

"It's goin' to be a race, Hap. The train has half an hour the start."

So the glossy, blue roadster went zooming down the lane. It was already eleven o'clock and Hap sped along as if trying to overtake ten-thirty a. m.

The train was due in Cedarville at noon. Hap managed to reach the station a minute and a half before it pulled in. His alert eyes soon caught a glimpse of Christy as she falteringly descended the platform steps.

"Christy!" She looked up and they stood face to face. He grasped her hands and said in low, clear tones, "The sun didn't go into a total eclipse, girlie."

She glanced up into his sparkling blue eyes with a dazed, wondering smile. Gently and quickly he ushered her into the glossy, blue roadster and they soon reached the wee, gray cottage of Heartha Wylie. Here they found the snow-path swept to the door.

Aunt Heartha was seemingly unsurprised to see them. She was accustomed to their various quirks and whims. At the same time they were totally unaware that Uncle Alec had interceded for Hap in a telephone conversation with Heartha—the most lengthy indulgence of the kind he had ever allowed himself.

"Aunt Heartha, will you ride over to Riverbend with Christy and me?" importuned Hap. "We want to show you some improvements on Grandma Word's old home."

Gallantly, almost tenderly, Hap escorted Aunt Heartha into the little blue roadster. She seemed as bright-eyed as a snowbird ensconced between the two. The white fields were a-glitter stretching to right and left on the highway.

It didn't seem long until they were back at the little homestead. Uncle Alec was still there. He stretched

short.title: Editorial
short.text:
VOL. XXV NOVEMBER, 1938 No. 11

Editorial

THE Relief Society Conference held in the Tabernacle October 1st and 2nd was one of the most inspiring and helpful in the history of the organization. The addresses were of unusual merit and the spirit of the meetings was uplifting and encouraging.

The reports given by the General Board members showed remarkable progress in all departments of Relief Society work. The membership has increased during the past year and the activities have been expanded and improved.

The visiting teachers' reports indicated that this important work is being carried on more effectively than ever before. The percentage of families visited has increased and the quality of the service rendered has been improved.

The work and business committee reports showed that the Relief Society is keeping pace with modern developments in the fields of health, education, and social service. The programs recommended for the coming year are practical and timely.

The Relief Society Magazine continues to grow in circulation and influence. It is now being read in many countries and is recognized as one of the outstanding women's magazines published anywhere.

We are grateful for the splendid cooperation of all Relief Society officers and members throughout the Church. Their loyalty and devotion make possible the continued growth and development of this great organization.
short.notes: This appears to be the main editorial content from the November 1938 issue, discussing the Relief Society Conference and organizational progress.

short.title: Questions and Problems
short.text:
Questions and Problems

(Deal only with as many as time permits.)

1. Why does labor in the mission field so generally aid our people to receive a testimony from the Holy Ghost?

2. Name all the functions of the Holy Ghost that you can. What is the difference between the Holy Ghost and the gift of the Holy Ghost? (Note: See Parry, Joseph Smith's Teachings, pp. 69-81.)

3. Explain the difference in the apostle Peter's courage before and after the resurrection. (Compare Matt. 26:69-75 with Acts 4:1-20.)

4. Let some one in the class present the Prophet Joseph Smith's discussion of the effect of the first Comforter or Holy Ghost upon a person. (See Parry, Joseph Smith's Teachings, pp. 21 f.)

* * * * *

The Deseret Book Company have on hand a few copies of "The Apostles of Jesus Christ" by the late Edward H. Anderson, and "Ancient Apostles," by David O. McKay, specially priced at seventy-nine cents per copy with ten cents additional cost for postage, and in the state of Utah, the sales tax also additional. These two books, published locally and written by our Church leaders, work nicely into the Theology course of study.

missing.title: Leadership Week
missing.text:
Leadership Week

The Relief Society is cooperating with the other auxiliaries in observing Leadership Week, February 13-20. The purpose is to give recognition to leadership and to inspire better leadership.

During this week each ward Relief Society should plan some special recognition of its leaders—past and present. This might take the form of a special program, a social hour, or some other appropriate observance.

The Relief Society has always been blessed with devoted, capable leadership. From the Prophet's wife, Emma Smith, who was the first president, down to the present time, the women who have served as officers have given generously of their time and talents.

We should be grateful for good leadership and should support our leaders loyally. We should also prepare ourselves to serve capably when called upon to do so.

Leadership is not a matter of position alone, but of influence. Every Relief Society sister has opportunities to lead—in her home, in her neighborhood, in the organizations with which she is associated. Let us all strive to be better leaders.

missing.title: The Law of the Lord
missing.author: Ruth May Fox
missing.text:
THE LAW OF THE LORD

By Ruth May Fox

THE law of the Lord is perfect, converting the soul: the testimony of the Lord is sure, making wise the simple.

The statutes of the Lord are right, rejoicing the heart: the commandment of the Lord is pure, enlightening the eyes.

The fear of the Lord is clean, enduring for ever: the judgments of the Lord are true and righteous altogether.

More to be desired are they than gold, yea, than much fine gold: sweeter also than honey and the honeycomb.

Moreover by them is thy servant warned: and in keeping of them there is great reward.

Who can understand his errors? cleanse thou me from secret faults.

Keep back thy servant also from presumptuous sins; let them not have dominion over me: then shall I be upright, and I shall be innocent from the great transgression.

Let the words of my mouth, and the meditation of my heart, be acceptable in thy sight, O Lord, my strength, and my redeemer.

—Psalm 19: 7-14.

missing.title: The Desert Is A-Bloom
missing.author: Lydia Hall
missing.text:
THE DESERT IS A-BLOOM

By Lydia Hall

The desert is a-bloom tonight
With stars like golden flowers,
And silver moonbeams weave their light
Through all the mystic hours.

The desert is a-bloom by day
With cactus bright and fair,
With yucca lifting spears of white
Into the crystal air.

The desert is a-bloom with dreams
Of those who journey here,
With visions born of solitude
And silence, sweet and clear.

The desert is a-bloom with God—
His presence fills the space
Where earth and sky in vastness meet
And man beholds His face.

missing.text:
HAPPENINGS

By Annie Wells Cannon

The Relief Society General Board held its regular monthly meeting June 2nd, with President Amy Brown Lyman presiding. All members of the Board were present except Sister Clarissa A. Beesley who is in California.

Sister Lyman reported on her recent trip to the Eastern States Mission where she visited Relief Society organizations in New York, Philadelphia, Washington, D. C., and other cities. She spoke at a number of meetings and was much pleased with the interest and enthusiasm shown by the sisters.

Sister Louise Y. Robison, first counselor, reported on her visit to the Northwestern States Mission where she attended conferences and spoke at Relief Society meetings in Seattle, Portland, and other cities.

Sister Julia A. F. Lund, second counselor, reported on her recent trip to California where she visited Relief Society organizations in Los Angeles, San Francisco, and other cities.

The Board discussed plans for the Relief Society conference to be held in October in connection with the general conference of the Church.

Sister Lyman announced that the Relief Society has been asked to furnish quilts for the new Deseret Hospital which is being built in Salt Lake City.

The Board voted to contribute $500 to the Church Building Fund.

Sister Belle S. Spafford, secretary of the General Board, reported that the Relief Society Magazine circulation has increased during the past year.

The meeting was closed with prayer by Sister Robison.

missing.title: Luxury
missing.author: Carla Wolfe
missing.text:
LUXURY

By Carla Wolfe

Luxury is not a thing of gold and silver,
Nor velvet soft, nor silk's bright sheen;
Luxury is not the gleaming satin
Of a rich and costly screen.

Luxury is not the sparkling crystal,
Nor the rarest, costliest gem;
Luxury is not fine linen
With a jeweled diadem.

Luxury is the quiet evening
When the children are in bed,
And you sit beside your husband
With his dear, familiar head

Resting near you in contentment,
While you darn his everyday socks,
And the old, reliable timepiece
On the mantelpiece softly talks.

Luxury is the baby's laughter
Ringing silver through the house;
Luxury is the morning singing
Of your busy little spouse.

Luxury is having plenty
Of the plain, substantial food;
Luxury is the peace and blessing
Of a simple neighborhood.

Luxury is not in having
All the costly things of earth;
Luxury is in the treasures
Of immeasurable worth—

Love and laughter, peace and plenty,
Health and hope and honest toil,
Friends whose hearts are true and tender—
These can never fade or spoil.

missing.title: She Makes An Art of Friendship
missing.author: E. C. C.
missing.text:
She Makes An Art of Friendship

By E. C. C.

She makes an art of friendship. In her hands
The common clay of human intercourse
Becomes a thing of beauty. She understands
The secret springs that move the human heart,
And knows the healing touch, the kindly word
That soothes the troubled spirit. She can start
A flame of hope where despair has stirred.

She has the gift of making others feel
That they are valued, that their lives have worth.
Her sympathy is genuine and real,
Her interest in their welfare proves her mirth
Is not mere surface brightness, but springs deep
From wells of understanding. She can keep
A confidence as sacred as a prayer.

She never wounds with careless, thoughtless speech,
Nor spreads abroad the faults that others show.
Her friendship is a sanctuary where each
May come for comfort when the heart hangs low.
She builds up courage, strengthens failing faith,
And by her very presence seems to breathe
New life into the souls that seek her care.

Oh, blessed are the women who possess
This precious gift of friendship! They can bring
To earth a touch of heaven's tenderness,
And make the desert places bloom and sing.
Their lives are benedictions, and their names
Are written in the book where honor claims
The noblest servants of the human race.
